original_abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) organic-inorganic lead halide perovskites have emerged in the past few years as a promising material for low-cost, high-efficiency optoelectronic devices. Spurred by this recent interest, several subclasses of halide perovskites such as two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites have begun to play a significant role in advancing the fundamental understanding of the structural, chemical, and physical properties of halide perovskites, which are technologically relevant. While the chemistry of these 2D materials is similar to that of the 3D halide perovskites, their layered structure with a hybrid organic-inorganic interface induces new emergent properties that can significantly or sometimes subtly be important. Synergistic properties can be realized in systems that combine different materials exhibiting different dimensionalities by exploiting their intrinsic compatibility. In many cases, the weaknesses of each material can be alleviated in heteroarchitectures. For example, 3D-2D halide perovskites can demonstrate novel behavior that neither material would be capable of separately. This review describes how the structural differences between 3D halide perovskites and 2D halide perovskites give rise to their disparate materials properties, discusses strategies for realizing mixed-dimensional systems of various architectures through solution-processing techniques, and presents a comprehensive outlook for the use of 3D-2D systems in solar cells. Finally, we investigate applications of 3D-2D systems beyond photovoltaics and offer our perspective on mixed-dimensional perovskite systems as semiconductor materials with unrivaled tunability, efficiency, and technologically relevant durability.
humanized_abstract: Recently three-dimensional (3D) organic-inorganic lead halide perovskites have attracted a huge interest in researches for potential use in a wide variety of low-cost and high-efficient optoelectronic devices such as solar cells and lasers. Consequently, several subclasses of halide perovskites have been synthesized like two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites and have greatly enhanced the knowledge on structure, chemistry, physical and therefore on the properties of halide perovskites and their uses for technology. Materials having a similar chemistry to the 3D halide perovskites exhibit unusual properties due to the two-dimensional structure and the hybrid organic-inorganic interface between the lamellae of the structure leading to a variety of emergent properties. Furthermore, the compatibility of various materials exhibiting diverse dimensionalities offers a mutual beneficial synergetic effect. Many heterostructures and different mixed-dimensional composite materials are consequently highly compatible and their weaknesses can be compensated. In this review we provide an insight in the structure functions relation within 3D and 2D halide perovskites as well as into new functions obtained in 3D-2D mixed dimensional materials obtained using solution-processing method. In conclusion we will discuss possible applications of 3D-2D structures and materials derived thereof in solar cells as well as other applications beyond photovoltaics and give our opinion concerning the benefits offered by mixed dimensional semiconductor perovskite materials in terms of versatility, efficiency and longer lasting lifetime than other conventional electronic materials and devices.
